metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default value and source of `_ACCRUING_SINCE` in the plan (e.g. 'copy the module-level constant from engine_b_backends' or 'set to `datetime(2025, 1, 1, tzinfo=timezone.utc)` per the lens convention') and add an assertion on its value or type to AC3.
evidence: Plan states: 'Unavailable(reason, _ACCRUING_SINCE)' with no definition of _ACCRUING_SINCE value or how it should be initialized
AC3 asserts Unavailable is returned but does not assert the accruing_since value, leaving it untestable and unspecified
findings.finding: The `_ACCRUING_SINCE` constant is referenced in the fail-closed contract (`Unavailable(reason, _ACCRUING_SINCE)`) but its value and source are never specified in the plan â€” an executing agent cannot determine whether to copy it from engine_b_backends, set it to a fixed datetime, or derive it dynamically.
findings.location: Concrete contracts â€” FAIL-CLOSED bullet; AC test_unavailable
scenarios: Executor picks an arbitrary datetime, diverging from the pattern used by engine_b_backends and creating silent schema drift in the Unavailable payload
AC3 passes even if _ACCRUING_SINCE is set to None or an incorrect epoch, because the test only checks the type returned
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Concrete contracts: '`_ACCRUING_SINCE` is a module-level datetime constant; set to the same value used in engine_b_backends (extract or reference it from there to ensure consistency).' Update AC3 to assert `isinstance(result.accruing_since, datetime)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) document that the CI environment installs scc so `test_live` is never skipped, OR (b) add a non-skippable AC that exercises the shell-out path via a bundled scc binary fixture or a recorded real output, and update the proving command accordingly.
evidence: AC2: 'PROVE (integration, skipif scc absent): pytest -q tests/unit/metrics/test_scc_loc.py::test_live -m integration'
The plan does not state that CI installs scc or that any non-skippable AC exercises the real shell-out path
All other ACs (test_single_parser, test_unavailable, test_empty_tree) use monkeypatching or module-attribute checks that bypass the real scc binary
findings.finding: The only AC that exercises the live `scc_loc.analyze` â†’ real scc shell-out path (AC2 / `test_live`) is guarded by `skipif scc absent` with no stated guarantee that scc is present in CI â€” meaning the new end-to-end code path (shelling out to scc, parsing real JSON output) may be entirely skipped in CI, leaving the live external-boundary path unverified per E6(f).
findings.location: Acceptance criteria â€” AC2 (test_live -m integration)
scenarios: In a CI environment without scc installed, test_live is skipped; the suite is green but the actual shell-out + JSON parsing path has never run, so a defect in the real invocation (wrong flags, JSON schema mismatch) ships undetected
The skipif guard satisfies E6(f)'s anti-FP only if the path has no live boundary â€” but shelling out to an external binary IS a live boundary, so the exception does not apply
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'CI installs scc via `apt-get install scc` / `brew install scc` / the project Dockerfile so `test_live` is never skipped.' Alternatively, add AC5: 'A fixture that replays a real scc JSON response through the full analyze() call path (without monkeypatching run itself) confirms the JSONâ†’AnalyzerResult pipeline end-to-end. PROVE: pytest -q tests/unit/metrics/test_scc_loc.py::test_real_json_fixture (no skipif).`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'FAIL-CLOSED' vs 'failing open to abstain' contradiction in the Concrete Contracts section by either removing the ambiguous parenthetical or rewording it to clarify that an abstained upstream result is treated as a failure that triggers Unavailable (i.e., the adapter closes what _metric_invoke leaves open).
evidence: Problem section: 'fails closed (Unavailable) when scc is unavailable'
Concrete contracts FAIL-CLOSED: 'mirrors _metric_invoke failing open to abstain'
The two phrases are in different sections and directly contradict each other on what 'fail' means.
findings.finding: The FAIL-CLOSED section in Concrete Contracts contains a direct terminological contradiction: the header declares 'FAIL-CLOSED' behavior, but the parenthetical says the adapter '(mirrors _metric_invoke failing open to abstain)'. Failing open and failing closed are opposites. This contradicts the Problem section's statement 'fails closed (Unavailable) when scc is unavailable' and will confuse an implementer about what behavior to implement.
findings.location: Concrete contracts section, FAIL-CLOSED bullet: '(mirrors _metric_invoke failing open to abstain)'
scenarios: Implementer reads 'FAIL-CLOSED' as the target behavior but then reads the parenthetical as saying the model is to 'fail open' like _metric_invoke and returns a zero/default instead of Unavailable on error.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the parenthetical with: '(NOTE: when run returns an abstained result â€” as _metric_invoke may â€” the adapter treats it as a failure and returns Unavailable, closing what the upstream left open)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether test_live belongs in tests/unit/ (with skipif guard) or tests/integration/ and update the AC prove command path accordingly so file location and test marker agree.
evidence: AC prove command uses path 'tests/unit/metrics/test_scc_loc.py'
Same AC marks the test '-m integration' and 'skipif scc absent'
Files Likely Touched lists only 'tests/unit/metrics/test_scc_loc.py' with no integration test file
findings.finding: The AC for test_live is marked '-m integration' and described as 'skipif scc absent', indicating it is an integration test, but it is placed in tests/unit/metrics/test_scc_loc.py. This cross-section contradiction between the Acceptance Criteria file path and the test marker means the test will not be collected when running the integration suite from the standard integration path, and will pollute the unit test run unless scc is installed.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria, test_live: 'pytest -q tests/unit/metrics/test_scc_loc.py::test_live -m integration'
scenarios: CI runs unit suite (no scc installed): test is skipped but the file is collected unnecessarily.
CI runs integration suite from a different root path: test_live is not found because it is in the unit directory.
A future developer splits unit and integration test directories and must move the test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback note (e.g., 'revert engine_b_backends.py to local _scc_files definition and delete analyzers/_scc.py') and/or add a behavioral-equivalence test asserting that the imported _scc_files produces identical output to the original for a known fixture.
evidence: Plan acknowledges the change: 're-import it in engine_b_backends'
Plan provides no rollback instruction if the import fails or if the verbatim extraction is not truly verbatim
AC test_single_parser verifies the module origin but does not verify behavioral equivalence of the extracted function
findings.finding: The plan modifies engine_b_backends.py (replacing a local definition of _scc_files with an import from analyzers/_scc.py) but provides no rollback or back-out path if the extraction introduces subtle drift. T4 requires an explicit rollback/back-out plan for changes to existing production code, not just acknowledgement.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section: 're-import it in engine_b_backends (one definition, no drift)'; Files Likely Touched: 'engine_b_backends.py (import _scc_files from the shared module)'
scenarios: Extracted _scc_files diverges from original during copy (e.g., different handling of missing keys); engine_b_backends now silently uses the wrong implementation with no rollback path defined.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'ROLLBACK: if _scc_files import causes engine_b_backends regression, revert the import line to the original local definition and reopen this work item. AC test_single_parser guards against module drift but not behavioral drift; add test_scc_files_equivalence asserting identical output on a shared fixture.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the cross-component import decision (grounding importing from metrics) in AGENTS.md or an ADR, or alternatively place `_scc_files` in a shared utility module (e.g. `rebar.metrics._scc_parser`) that both grounding and metrics can import without layering ambiguity.
evidence: 'extract `_scc_files` VERBATIM into `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/_scc.py` and re-import it in engine_b_backends'
No ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update is listed in 'Files likely touched'
Grounding backends importing from the metrics/analyzers sub-package is a non-trivial dependency-direction decision with no documented justification
findings.finding: The plan introduces a cross-component import from `rebar.grounding.engine_b_backends` into `rebar.metrics.analyzers._scc` â€” a metrics-layer module â€” reversing or crossing the expected dependency direction without documenting the architectural rationale or mitigating the coupling.
findings.location: Approach & Rationale / Files likely touched â€” engine_b_backends.py
scenarios: A future developer working on the metrics package changes `_scc_files`'s return schema to serve new metrics needs, inadvertently breaking the grounding backend that silently depends on it.
The dependency direction (grounding â†’ metrics) conflicts with a layering rule elsewhere in the codebase, creating confusion about which package owns the parser.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `## Architecture note` entry to AGENTS.md (or a lightweight ADR) explaining why `_scc_files` lives in `rebar.metrics.analyzers._scc` and is re-imported by the grounding backend. If a layering rule already exists that forbids groundingâ†’metrics imports, move the parser to a shared `rebar.metrics._scc_shared` or `rebar.tools._scc` module instead.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `scc_loc.analyze` emits a structured log (e.g. `logger.warning('scc unavailable: %s', reason)`) or increments a counter when returning Unavailable, and add an assertion in `test_unavailable` that this log/metric is emitted.
evidence: 'a missing binary, non-zero exit, unparseable JSON, or an abstained run -> Unavailable(reason, _ACCRUING_SINCE)'
No mention of logger.warning/error, metrics counter, or structured log in any section
The Acceptance Criteria for test_unavailable only checks the return value, not that a diagnostic event is emitted
findings.finding: No observability is specified for the scc shell-out failure path: the plan returns `Unavailable(reason, _ACCRUING_SINCE)` but does not call for any log, metric, or trace emission when scc is absent or fails, leaving operators without a signal to diagnose missing LOC data.
findings.location: FAIL-CLOSED section / Acceptance criteria â€” test_unavailable
scenarios: scc is silently removed from the deployment environment; LOC data disappears from dashboards with no log entry; operators cannot distinguish 'no files' from 'scc broken' without grepping Unavailable objects at runtime.
A transient PATH misconfiguration causes repeated Unavailable returns that are invisible in monitoring until a user reports missing metrics.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)` to `scc_loc.py` and emit `logger.warning('scc_loc: returning Unavailable: %s', reason)` in the Unavailable branch. Optionally record a counter via the existing rebar metrics infrastructure if one exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `Location` values from `_scc_files` are normalized to POSIX-style forward-slash separators (e.g. `Path(entry['Location']).as_posix()`) before being used as `files` dict keys, so the payload is correct on Windows.
evidence: Plan quote: 'LOC PAYLOAD produced by this adapter: AnalyzerResult.loc = {"files": {<relpath:str>: <loc:int>}, "max_loc": <int>} where files is built from _scc_files'
Plan quote: '_scc_files(data)->list[dict]; :410-420 read entry.get("Code") / entry.get("Location")'
Plan quote: 'extract _scc_files VERBATIM into analyzers/_scc.py' â€” no normalization of Location values is mentioned
scc on Windows outputs native backslash separators in its JSON Location field; rebar's support matrix includes Windows platform
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the `files` dict is keyed by `<relpath:str>` values taken from `_scc_files`'s `entry.get("Location")` â€” extracted verbatim from existing code that reads the field raw. On Windows, `scc` emits `Location` values with backslash path separators (e.g. `src\foo\bar.py`). The plan specifies no normalization step, so the `files` dict will be keyed with OS-native separators on Windows, while rebar consumers and the code_health lens are expected to use POSIX-style relative paths throughout.
findings.location: Concrete contracts â€” LOC PAYLOAD: `"files": {<relpath:str>: <loc:int>}` built from `_scc_files` reading `entry.get("Location")`; Approach â€” "extracted verbatim"
scenarios: Windows platform: a developer or CI run on Windows where scc reports `Location` as `src\metrics\foo.py`; the `files` dict is keyed `src\metrics\foo.py`; any downstream consumer (code_health lens, AnalyzerResult reader) looking up a POSIX path `src/metrics/foo.py` gets a miss â€” silently returning no LOC data for every file.
findings.suggested_fix: In `scc_loc.py`'s `analyze()`, after calling `_scc_files(data)`, normalize each path: `rel = Path(entry['Location']).as_posix()` before inserting into `files`. This is a one-liner and keeps `_scc_files` itself verbatim while fixing the separator issue in the adapter layer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test AC (e.g. `test_malformed_json`) that patches `run` to return exit-code 0 with non-JSON stdout and asserts `analyze()` returns `Unavailable`, covering the 'unparseable JSON' branch named in the FAIL-CLOSED contract.
evidence: FAIL-CLOSED spec: 'a missing binary, non-zero exit, unparseable JSON, or an abstained run -> Unavailable'
test_unavailable AC: 'monkeypatched run returns an abstained/failed result' â€” covers runner-level failure only, not parse-level failure
No AC exists for: runner returns exit 0 with non-JSON stdout
findings.finding: The FAIL-CLOSED contract explicitly names 'unparseable JSON' as a failure mode that must return Unavailable, but no AC covers the scenario where scc exits 0 yet emits invalid JSON (e.g. truncated/malformed output). The test_unavailable AC only patches the runner to return an abstained/failed result, which does not exercise the JSON parse failure branch.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria â€” test_unavailable AC; Concrete contracts â€” FAIL-CLOSED bullet
scenarios: scc binary present and exits 0 but writes malformed JSON (e.g. partial output, encoding error, future format change) â€” the JSON parse exception path in analyze() is never exercised by the test suite, so a bug there would not be caught
findings.suggested_fix: Add to test_scc_loc.py: a parametrized or standalone test where the monkeypatched runner returns (returncode=0, stdout='not-json-{') and assert isinstance(result, Unavailable). This directly proves the JSON parse guard in analyze() works.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `_scc_files` extraction to `analyzers/_scc.py` is justified now (cite a third existing or in-scope consumer), OR defer the extraction and have `scc_loc.py` import `_scc_files` directly from `engine_b_backends` until a third consumer materialises.
evidence: grep hit: `_scc_files` defined at engine_b_backends.py:429 â€” exactly 1 existing call-site at :412
Plan creates 1 new consumer (scc_loc.py) â†’ total 2 consumers after this ticket
Plan justification: 'single parser, zero schema drift' â€” framed as preventing future drift, not serving a current done-definition
Rule-of-Three requires â‰Ą3 call-sites for a shared-module extraction to be non-premature
Simpler alternative: import `_scc_files` directly from `engine_b_backends` into `scc_loc.py`, or copy the trivial 6-line function; defer extraction until a third consumer exists
findings.finding: The extraction of `_scc_files` into `analyzers/_scc.py` as a shared module is a premature abstraction: the grep-verified call-site count is exactly ONE (engine_b_backends.py:412), and after this plan it reaches only TWO (engine_b_backends re-import + scc_loc.py) â€” below the Rule-of-Three threshold. The plan's stated rationale ('zero schema drift') is a hypothetical future benefit with no third consumer in scope.
findings.location: Approach & rationale â€” 'REUSE it: extract `_scc_files` VERBATIM into `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/_scc.py` and re-import it in engine_b_backends'
scenarios: A new shared module `analyzers/_scc.py` is created and maintained indefinitely to serve only two consumers, adding indirection with no measurable deduplication benefit at current scale.
If the `_scc_files` schema changes, the same two-site update is needed regardless of whether the function lives in a shared module or is inlined.
findings.suggested_fix: In `scc_loc.py`, import `_scc_files` directly: `from rebar.grounding.engine_b_backends import _scc_files`. Remove the intermediate `analyzers/_scc.py` module. When a third consumer appears (e.g. a complexity or duplication analyzer), perform the extraction at that point.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/__init__.py` to 'Files likely touched' (new package needs an `__init__.py` to be importable).
evidence: Plan lists only `_scc.py` and `scc_loc.py` under `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/`, with no `__init__.py`
Confirmed: `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/` does not exist in repo; the parallel `adapters/` package has an `__init__.py` (adapters/__init__.py verified)
AC: `pytest -q tests/unit/metrics/test_scc_loc.py::test_single_parser` asserts `engine_b_backends._scc_files.__module__ == 'rebar.metrics.analyzers._scc'` â€” requires the package to be importable
findings.finding: The new `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/` package requires an `__init__.py` to be importable, but this file is absent from 'Files likely touched'. Without it, `from rebar.metrics.analyzers._scc import _scc_files` will raise `ModuleNotFoundError` and the AC proving command (`engine_b_backends._scc_files.__module__ == 'rebar.metrics.analyzers._scc'`) will fail.
findings.location: Files likely touched
scenarios: Implementer creates `_scc.py` and `scc_loc.py` but forgets `__init__.py`; all imports fail at runtime and in CI
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/__init__.py` to the file list with note: `new: package init for the analyzers sub-package`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge (or justify) the new `rebar.metrics â†’ rebar.grounding.harness` import direction introduced by the `run=harness.run_tool` default in `scc_loc.py`.

Grep of src/rebar/metrics/ for 'grounding' returns zero matches â€” no existing metrics module imports from grounding
harness.run_tool confirmed at rebar/grounding/harness.py:129
findings.finding: The entry-point signature `scc_loc.analyze(repo_root, *, run=<runner>=harness.run_tool)` introduces a new cross-package import (`rebar.metrics.analyzers.scc_loc` importing `rebar.grounding.harness`) that currently does not exist anywhere in `rebar.metrics`. No existing `rebar/metrics/**` module imports from `rebar.grounding` (confirmed by grep). The plan does not acknowledge this new dependency direction or verify it is permitted by any import contracts.

scenarios: An import-graph contract or future linter rule bans metricsâ†’grounding imports; CI breaks
The `scc_loc` module must import `harness` at module scope to supply the default, creating a novel cross-subsystem edge

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the value and source of `_ACCRUING_SINCE` in `scc_loc.py` (e.g., a hardcoded ISO-8601 string matching the ticket creation date, or a convention shared across all analyzers).
evidence: Plan: `Unavailable(reason, _ACCRUING_SINCE)` used as if `_ACCRUING_SINCE` is a pre-defined module constant
Dep ticket 3b30 creates `AnalyzerResult`/`Unavailable` but does not define `_ACCRUING_SINCE` for scc_loc
Dep ticket 9597 does not define it either
`Unavailable` signature confirmed: `reason: str, accruing_since: str` (registry.py:81-89)
findings.finding: `_ACCRUING_SINCE` is referenced as a named constant in the fail-closed contract (`Unavailable(reason, _ACCRUING_SINCE)`) but its origin, type, and initialization are unspecified anywhere in the plan or its dependency tickets. `Unavailable` (from `rebar.metrics.registry`) takes `accruing_since: str` (ISO-8601). Without specifying where this constant comes from, the implementer has no contract to follow.
findings.location: Concrete contracts â€” FAIL-CLOSED
scenarios: Implementer must invent a value for `_ACCRUING_SINCE` (hardcoded date string? dynamic timestamp?), leading to inconsistency with other analyzers

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify where and how `Location` values from `_scc_files` are relativized to `repo_root` before being used as keys in `AnalyzerResult.loc['files']` (e.g. `Path(entry['Location']).relative_to(repo_root)` in `scc_loc.analyze`, NOT inside the shared `_scc_files` parser).
evidence: Plan: 'files is built from _scc_files and max_loc = max(files.values(), default=0)' with key type '<relpath:str>'
Confirmed: engine_b_backends.py:376 calls scc with str(repo_root) as positional arg; scc emits absolute paths in Location field
engine_b_backends.py:421 uses entry.get('Location', '?') directly (no relativization) â€” existing code does not relativize, so verbatim extraction will yield absolute paths
Plan says extract _scc_files 'VERBATIM' and build files from it â€” no path-relativization step specified
findings.finding: The plan promises `files` keys are `<relpath:str>` but scc's `Location` field is an absolute path when the tool is invoked as `scc --format json <repo_root>` (confirmed by the existing engine_b_backends usage at :421 which already uses `entry.get('Location', '?')` without relativization). The plan says to extract `_scc_files` verbatim and use its `Location` directly â€” no relativization step is specified anywhere in the adapter's data-flow, leaving the contract (`{<relpath>: <loc>}`) unachievable as written.
findings.location: Concrete contracts section â€” 'LOC PAYLOAD' definition; Approach section â€” 'extract _scc_files VERBATIM'
scenarios: scc_loc.analyze('/repo') returns {'files': {'/repo/src/foo.py': 100}} instead of {'files': {'src/foo.py': 100}}, breaking any downstream consumer that computes relative paths for display or diffing
findings.suggested_fix: In `scc_loc.analyze`, after calling `_scc_files(data)`, compute `relpath = str(Path(entry['Location']).relative_to(repo_root))` for each entry; keep `_scc_files` unchanged (it returns raw dicts). Document this step in the Concrete contracts section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (and document in the plan) that the import-linter configuration permits `grounding.engine_b_backends` importing from `metrics.analyzers`, OR consider an alternative sharing strategy (e.g., keep `_scc_files` in a third, dependency-free utility module under `rebar.grounding` or `rebar._scc_parse`) that avoids the layering inversion.
evidence: Plan: 'engine_b_backends imports it from here' (the new analyzers/_scc.py location)
engine_b_backends.py is in src/rebar/grounding/; new _scc.py is in src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/
scc_loc.py per the plan calls harness.run_tool (grounding) â€” so metrics/analyzers â†’ grounding already exists
The resulting import graph: grounding.engine_b_backends â†’ metrics.analyzers._scc AND metrics.analyzers.scc_loc â†’ grounding.harness
Ticket 9597 explicitly references import-linter optionality constraints; project's layering treats grounding as a lower-level subsystem
findings.finding: The plan's extraction strategy â€” moving `_scc_files` to `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/_scc.py` and having `src/rebar/grounding/engine_b_backends.py` import it from there â€” creates a cross-layer import in the direction `grounding â†’ metrics/analyzers`. The new `scc_loc.py` itself will import from `rebar.grounding.harness` (to call `harness.run_tool`), making `metrics/analyzers` a consumer of `grounding`. Having `grounding` also import back from `metrics/analyzers` may violate the project's import-linter rules (which ticket 9597 references as the 'optionality ban-list') and creates a layering inversion that is not acknowledged or justified in the plan.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section; Files likely touched â€” engine_b_backends.py import change
scenarios: Import-linter CI check flags the new groundingâ†’metrics import as a forbidden cross-layer dependency and the build fails
A developer adding a metrics import to _scc.py later creates a genuine circular import, which is now latent

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fetch or reconstruct rebar:3b30-2168-ba16-41fc's plan and confirm the leaf's AnalyzerResult.loc shape ({"files": {...}, "max_loc": int}) is explicitly sanctioned by the parent; if the parent specifies a different shape, realign the leaf (or update the parent first and re-review).
evidence: show_ticket('rebar:3b30-2168-ba16-41fc') returned 'Ticket not found' â€” parent plan is inaccessible, containment cannot be confirmed
Leaf plan defines AnalyzerResult.loc shape independently: '{"files": {<relpath:str>: <loc:int>}, "max_loc": <int>}' â€” this is a deliverable the parent (3b30) owns

Sibling cache .rebar/cache/plan-review/0734-ceae-be55-4fa7/b259fed4083de45afa18.json quotes 3b30 as defining 'AnalyzerResult (fields loc/complexity/duplication, each optional=None when unsupported)' â€” loc as a plain optional field with no sub-attributes, contradicting the leaf's dict-with-max_loc shape

The leaf's own AC section (test_live fixture) asserts 'AnalyzerResult.loc == {"files": {...}, "max_loc": 850}' and test_empty asserts 'AnalyzerResult(loc={"files":{},"max_loc":0})' â€” both presuppose the parent's contract shape
findings.finding: The parent ticket rebar:3b30-2168-ba16-41fc (owner of the AnalyzerResult / Unavailable / resolve_analyzer contracts) is not fetchable from the ticket system, making G7 leaf-parent containment unverifiable. The leaf's 'Concrete contracts' section independently specifies the internal shape of AnalyzerResult.loc as {"files": {<relpath>: <int>}, "max_loc": <int>} â€” a deliverable the parent owns. Secondary evidence from sibling plan-review caches quotes 3b30 as defining AnalyzerResult with plain 'loc/complexity/duplication' optional fields (no sub-structure), which would contradict the leaf's nested dict shape. If the parent and leaf disagree on the loc shape, the leaf is redefining a parent deliverable â€” a G7 violation where the parent wins.
findings.location: Concrete contracts section: 'LOC PAYLOAD produced by this adapter: AnalyzerResult.loc = {"files": {<relpath:str>: <loc:int>}, "max_loc": <int>}'; Dependencies section: 'Dependencies: 3b30, 9597, e7a0'
scenarios: Parent 3b30 ships AnalyzerResult with loc as a plain optional field (e.g. int or None), but the leaf builds and tests against loc as a dict â€” at integration time the type contract breaks.

Parent 3b30 defines max_loc as a top-level field on AnalyzerResult (not nested inside loc), but the leaf embeds it inside loc â€” downstream consumers (ticket 0734) cannot access it via the path either ticket specifies.
findings.suggested_fix: If the parent does not yet specify the loc sub-structure, update the parent ticket's plan to define AnalyzerResult.loc as {"files": {str: int}, "max_loc": int} before merging this leaf â€” then re-review both. Do not leave the shape defined only in the leaf, which is not the authoritative owner of AnalyzerResult.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly how `scc_loc.analyze` discovers the scc binary and whether it imports from `rebar.grounding` â€” document the dependency direction as intentional or provide a coupling-free alternative (e.g., accept `binary: str | None` as a parameter, defaulting via a helper that can stay in `grounding`).
evidence: Plan states: 'ENTRY POINT: `scc_loc.analyze(repo_root, *, run=<runner>=harness.run_tool) -> AnalyzerResult | Unavailable`'
Directory listing shows `src/rebar/metrics/` contains no imports from `rebar.grounding` â€” the harness is exclusively used by `rebar/grounding/*.py` modules
Confirmed `harness.run_tool` signature requires `backend: str` kwarg (harness.py:129-137); plan does not specify how the metrics adapter threads this through its injected `run` callable

Binary discovery (`engine_b._resolve_binary`, engine_b.py:84-89) lives in `rebar.grounding.engine_b`; the plan is silent on whether `scc_loc.py` calls it, duplicates `shutil.which`, or relies on the runner abstraction to encapsulate it â€” each choice has different coupling implications
findings.finding: The new `scc_loc.analyze()` in `rebar.metrics.analyzers` will import `harness.run_tool` from `rebar.grounding.harness` â€” introducing a new cross-package dependency direction (`metrics` â†’ `grounding`) that does not currently exist. The plan does not acknowledge or justify this boundary crossing, nor does it address how binary discovery (`_resolve_binary` / `shutil.which`) will be handled without also importing from `rebar.grounding.engine_b`.
findings.location: Approach & rationale / ENTRY POINT contract / Files likely touched: scc_loc.py
scenarios: If `scc_loc.py` imports `harness` directly, `rebar.metrics` gains a hard dependency on `rebar.grounding`, which may violate layering constraints and complicate testing (tests must mock grounding internals).
If `scc_loc.py` also imports `engine_b._resolve_binary` for binary discovery, the metrics package is now coupled to two grounding internals with no stated policy on this.

If the injected `run` callable is expected to fully encapsulate binary resolution (i.e., callers pre-resolve and wrap), the plan's default `run=harness.run_tool` is wrong â€” `harness.run_tool` still needs a binary path, not just a tool name.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `backend=` (and any other mandatory `harness.run_tool` keyword args) is supplied through the `run=` injection interface â€” e.g. by fixing the default to a partial/lambda (`run=lambda cmd: harness.run_tool(cmd, backend=BACKEND_METRIC)`) or by requiring the adapter to always pass `backend=` explicitly and documenting the runner callable's expected signature.
evidence: harness.py:129-137: `def run_tool(cmd: Sequence[str], *, backend: str, timeout: float | None = None, ...)` â€” `backend` is mandatory, no default
Plan text: 'ENTRY POINT: `scc_loc.analyze(repo_root, *, run=<runner>=harness.run_tool) -> AnalyzerResult | Unavailable`' â€” no mention of how `backend=` is supplied through the injectable interface
engine_b_backends.py:376 shows the production call pattern: `harness.run_tool([binary, "--format", "json", str(repo_root)], backend=BACKEND_METRIC)` â€” `backend` is always explicitly passed
Verified (observed): harness.RunResult and harness.run_tool signature confirmed from source
findings.finding: The plan declares `run=<runner>=harness.run_tool` as the injectable default, but `harness.run_tool` requires `backend: str` as a mandatory keyword argument (confirmed at harness.py:132). If `scc_loc.analyze` calls `run(cmd, ...)` without forwarding a `backend=` keyword, the call will raise `TypeError` at runtime. The plan does not specify how `backend` is supplied when using the default runner, leaving a latent integration gap between the proposed injection interface and the actual harness contract.
findings.location: Concrete contracts â€” ENTRY POINT: `scc_loc.analyze(repo_root, *, run=<runner>=harness.run_tool)`
scenarios: Developer implements `scc_loc.analyze` using `run(cmd)` without `backend=`, causing `TypeError: run_tool() missing 1 required keyword-only argument: 'backend'` on every call with the default runner.
Test author writes a monkeypatched `run` stub with only `(cmd)` signature, which passes unit tests but diverges from the real harness interface, masking the integration bug.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the entry-point contract to: `scc_loc.analyze(repo_root, *, run=lambda cmd: harness.run_tool(cmd, backend="scc-loc")) -> AnalyzerResult | Unavailable`. This makes the default a zero-arg-on-`backend` partial that matches the harness signature, keeps injection clean for tests, and prevents the `TypeError` at runtime.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in `scc_loc.analyze()` where and how `entry['Location']` (absolute path from scc) is converted to a repo-relative path before being inserted as a `files` dict key, since `_scc_files` extracted verbatim does not perform this conversion.
evidence: Plan: 'LOC PAYLOAD produced by this adapter: AnalyzerResult.loc = {"files": {<relpath:str>: <loc:int>}, "max_loc": <int>}'
Plan: 'SHARED PARSER: analyzers/_scc.py: _scc_files(data) â€¦ extracted verbatim'
engine_b_backends.py:433: `for f in (lang.get('Files') or [])` â€” returns raw file dicts
engine_b_backends.py:422: `location={'file': entry.get('Location', '?')}` â€” Location used as-is (absolute path from scc)
ABSENCE: no rule in the plan specifying `os.path.relpath(entry['Location'], repo_root)` or equivalent inside `scc_loc.analyze()`
findings.finding: The output schema mandates relative paths as `files` dict keys (`<relpath:str>`), but the plan also mandates that `_scc_files` is extracted VERBATIM from `engine_b_backends.py:429-436`. The actual `_scc_files` returns raw `entry.get('Location')` values, which scc emits as absolute paths â€” the existing backend uses them as absolute paths too (line 422). No processing rule is specified in `scc_loc.py` for converting absolute Location values to repo-relative paths, leaving the absoluteâ†’relative transformation step unspecified.
findings.location: Concrete contracts section: `LOC PAYLOAD` schema + `SHARED PARSER` clause; engine_b_backends.py:412-422
scenarios: Implementer extracts `_scc_files` verbatim and uses `entry['Location']` directly as the dict key â†’ payload contains absolute paths, violating the relpath schema contract and breaking any consumer that expects repo-relative keys.
AC test `test_live` may pass if the fixture happens to use relative paths, masking the bug in production.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Concrete contracts section, add: '`scc_loc.analyze()` converts each `entry["Location"]` to a relative path via `os.path.relpath(entry["Location"], repo_root)` before inserting into `files`; this conversion lives in `scc_loc.py`, NOT in `_scc_files` (which remains a pure parser).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete ISO-8601 value for `_ACCRUING_SINCE` in `scc_loc.py` (e.g. the ticket's creation timestamp or the sprint start date), matching the convention used in `event_metrics.py` and `git_metrics.py`.
evidence: Plan: 'Unavailable(reason, _ACCRUING_SINCE)' â€” symbol used but never defined
event_metrics.py:313: `_ACCRUING_SINCE = "2026-01-01T00:00:00+00:00"`
git_metrics.py:236: `_ACCRUING_SINCE = "2026-01-01T00:00:00+00:00"`
ABSENCE: no line in the plan specifying the ISO-8601 timestamp value for `scc_loc._ACCRUING_SINCE`
findings.finding: `_ACCRUING_SINCE` is referenced in the FAIL-CLOSED clause as the second argument to `Unavailable(reason, _ACCRUING_SINCE)`, implying it is a module-level constant in `scc_loc.py`, but the plan never specifies its value or mandates where it must be defined. Every existing metrics module assigns a concrete ISO-8601 string to this constant; the plan leaves it completely unspecified.
findings.location: Concrete contracts â€” FAIL-CLOSED clause; compare src/rebar/metrics/event_metrics.py:313, git_metrics.py:236
scenarios: Implementer must guess the correct `accruing_since` timestamp, producing a value inconsistent with when the scc signal actually began accruing.
The AC test `test_unavailable` checks only the type of return value, not `accruing_since`, so an incorrect value will pass tests but produce wrong provenance metadata.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `resolve_analyzer(lang)` is actually used by this adapter (and if so, where in the `analyze()` flow); if it is not used, remove it from the Grounding section to avoid misleading implementers.

Search for `resolve_analyzer` across entire src/rebar tree: zero matches

findings.finding: `resolve_analyzer(lang)` is cited in the Grounding section as coming from the prerequisite ticket `3b30-2168-ba16-41fc`, but it is never referenced in any AC, entry-point signature, or implementation detail in this plan. No such symbol exists anywhere in the current codebase (search returned zero matches). It is a dangling referenced symbol with no defined role in this adapter.
findings.location: Grounding section: '`resolve_analyzer(lang)` from prerequisite `[rebar:3b30-2168-ba16-41fc]`'
scenarios: An implementer reads the Grounding section and imports `resolve_analyzer` into `scc_loc.py` expecting it to be needed, introducing an unused/broken import.
If `resolve_analyzer` IS required by the prerequisite ticket's `AnalyzerResult` construction protocol, omitting it from the implementation will cause a runtime failure.

findings.finding: `accruing_since` is described as a required module-level constant in the Approach section ('`accruing_since` = a module-level constant matching existing reader modules') but no acceptance criterion verifies its presence or correctness, leaving this deliverable uncovered.

findings.finding: The Approach names `accruing_since` as a required deliverable ('a module-level constant matching existing reader modules') but no Acceptance Criterion verifies its presence or value, leaving it an uncovered deliverable.
findings.location: Approach section ('accruing_since = a module-level constant matching existing reader modules') vs. Acceptance criteria (no AC for accruing_since)
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: `pytest -q tests/unit/metrics/test_scc_loc.py::test_accruing_since` asserting `isinstance(scc_loc.accruing_since, str) and scc_loc.accruing_since != ''` and ideally asserting its value matches a known reader module constant.

findings.finding: AC3's proof command says 'monkeypatched runner raises FileNotFoundError', but harness.run_tool() NEVER raises FileNotFoundError â€” it catches it and returns RunResult(abstain_reason='no_tool'). The later 'Tool-availability' section contradicts AC3 by correctly describing the abstained-RunResult path. An implementer following AC3 verbatim would patch at the subprocess/OS level rather than patching harness.run_tool to return an abstained result, testing the wrong layer and leaving scc_loc's own Unavailable path untested.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria â€” AC3 proof command: 'monkeypatched runner raises FileNotFoundError'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC3's proof command with: 'PROVE: pytest -q tests/unit/metrics/test_scc_loc.py::test_absent_scc_unavailable (monkeypatched harness.run_tool returns RunResult(backend=..., completed=False, abstain_reason="no_tool"))' â€” consistent with how every other backend test in the codebase patches the harness rather than the OS.

findings.finding: The AC3 proving command specifies 'monkeypatched runner raises FileNotFoundError', but the established in-repo scc shell-out pattern uses `harness.run_tool()` (engine_b_backends.py:376), which internally catches FileNotFoundError and converts it to a `RunResult(abstain_reason='no_tool')` â€” it never propagates FileNotFoundError to callers. If `scc_loc.py` correctly uses `harness.run_tool` (the repo's fail-open pattern), the AC3 test as written will fail or require patching at the wrong boundary. If `scc_loc.py` uses raw subprocess instead, it bypasses the proven fail-open harness and loses timeout/reap behavior.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria â€“ AC3 (scc absent / non-zero exit); Approach & rationale section
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC3 test description with: 'monkeypatch `harness.run_tool` to return `RunResult(completed=False, abstain_reason="no_tool", ...)` and assert the result is `Unavailable`.' In scc_loc.py, shell via `harness.run_tool([binary, "--format", "json", str(root)], backend="scc_loc")` and treat `res.abstained` as the Unavailable trigger.

findings.finding: The first AC block's prove command for `test_absent_scc_unavailable` specifies 'monkeypatched runner raises FileNotFoundError' as the test mechanism, but `harness.run_tool` (harness.py:173-182) already catches `FileNotFoundError` internally and converts it to `RunResult(abstain_reason='no_tool')` â€” it never re-raises to callers. This is a cargo-cult test spec: the `FileNotFoundError`-raise pattern is copied from a naive subprocess mental model, not from the actual harness contract. The later 'Tool-availability' section correctly specifies 'monkeypatched runner returns an abstained/failed result', creating an internal contradiction in the ACs.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria â€” AC bullet 3: `test_absent_scc_unavailable` (monkeypatched runner raises FileNotFoundError)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'monkeypatched runner raises FileNotFoundError' with 'monkeypatched `harness.run_tool` returns `RunResult(backend=..., completed=False, abstain_reason="no_tool", ...)` ' in AC bullet 3, making it consistent with the Tool-availability section's `test_unavailable` spec.

findings.finding: AC1's first `test_absent_scc_unavailable` test is specified as '(monkeypatched runner raises FileNotFoundError)', but the actual harness (`harness.run_tool`, harness.py:175) catches `FileNotFoundError` internally and returns `RunResult(completed=False, abstain_reason='no_tool')` â€” it never propagates the exception. The premise that `scc_loc.analyze` would ever see a raw `FileNotFoundError` from the runner is false given the verified harness contract.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria block 1: `test_absent_scc_unavailable` â€” '(monkeypatched runner raises FileNotFoundError)'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `test_absent_scc_unavailable` entry from AC block 1 (it duplicates `test_unavailable` in the tool-availability section with an incorrect trigger mechanism), or respecify it as: 'monkeypatched `harness.run_tool` returns `RunResult(completed=False, abstain_reason="no_tool")` â†’ `Unavailable`.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts that the absent-scc signal for AC4 is a FileNotFoundError from a 'monkeypatched runner', but the established in-repo scc shell-out pattern uses rebar.grounding.harness.run_tool which catches FileNotFoundError and converts it internally to abstain_reason='no_tool' â€” never surfacing FileNotFoundError to callers. The plan does not specify which execution boundary scc_loc.analyze() uses, leaving an unresolved assertion: if the new analyzer reuses harness.run_tool as claimed, no FileNotFoundError ever reaches the analyzer and the AC4 test contract targets a non-existent exception surface.
findings.location: AC4 / Approach & rationale ('reuses the established in-repo scc shell-out pattern') / tests/unit/metrics/test_scc_loc.py::test_absent_scc_unavailable
findings.suggested_fix: If reusing harness.run_tool (preferred for consistency): update AC4's proving description to 'monkeypatched harness.run_tool returning RunResult(abstained=True, abstain_reason="no_tool")' and document that scc_loc translates an abstained RunResult into Unavailable. If a direct subprocess call is chosen instead, add a sentence to the Approach section explicitly noting this deviation from the established harness pattern and specify the timeout/reaping contract.

findings.finding: AC3's proving command says 'monkeypatched runner raises FileNotFoundError', but harness.run_tool (the established integration pattern used at engine_b_backends.py:376) internally catches FileNotFoundError and converts it to RunResult(abstain_reason='no_tool') â€” it never re-raises. If scc_loc.py follows the same pattern, monkeypatching for a raw FileNotFoundError tests the wrong boundary and the test can only pass if scc_loc.py bypasses harness entirely and uses raw subprocess, losing timeout/reaping guarantees. The plan does not specify which runner scc_loc.py uses, leaving this integration path ambiguous.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria â€” AC3 ('monkeypatched runner raises FileNotFoundError')
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC3 to: 'scc absent -> `Unavailable`. PROVE: monkeypatch `harness.run_tool` to return `RunResult(backend=â€¦, completed=False, abstain_reason="no_tool")` and assert the returned value is an `Unavailable` instance (not a crash and not zeros).'

findings.finding: The first AC block's `test_absent_scc_unavailable` tests the unavailable path via `FileNotFoundError`, but the Tool-availability section explicitly states the pattern does NOT rely on a raised FileNotFoundError (it uses abstained/failed harness results). The second AC block's `test_unavailable` then re-covers the same scenario with the correct mechanism. This creates a direct cross-section contradiction: two ACs for the same scenario specify mutually exclusive triggering mechanisms.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria block 1 (`test_absent_scc_unavailable`) vs Tool-availability section (`test_unavailable`)
findings.suggested_fix: Delete `test_absent_scc_unavailable` from the first AC block (it is superseded by `test_unavailable` in the Tool-availability section). If a FileNotFoundError path genuinely exists as a fallback, document it explicitly and keep both tests with distinct names and non-overlapping trigger conditions.

findings.finding: The fallback for a zero-exit scc that emits empty or unparseable JSON output is not specified for `scc_loc.py`. AC3 only covers `FileNotFoundError` (scc absent) and non-zero exit code; the JSON-parse-error path exists in `engine_b_backends` but the plan does not indicate whether `scc_loc.analyze()` should propagate an exception, return `Unavailable`, or silently return an empty result.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria â€” AC3: 'scc absent / non-zero exit -> Unavailable(reason, accruing_since)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'zero-exit scc with empty or unparseable JSON output -> Unavailable(reason="scc output unreadable", accruing_since=...) â€” PROVE: monkeypatched runner returns returncode=0 but stdout="{bad"'.

findings.finding: The plan inconsistently describes the shape of `AnalyzerResult`: the AC section uses `AnalyzerResult.loc.max_loc` (implying `max_loc` is nested inside a `loc` sub-object), while the Tool-availability section uses `AnalyzerResult(loc={}, max_loc=0)` (implying `max_loc` is a top-level field alongside `loc`). Since `AnalyzerResult` does not yet exist in the codebase (gated on dep 3b30), an implementer reading only this plan cannot determine the correct shape.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria section (AC line: `AnalyzerResult whose .loc.max_loc equals`) vs Tool-availability section (`AnalyzerResult(loc={}, max_loc=0)`)
findings.suggested_fix: If `AnalyzerResult.loc` is a typed sub-object (e.g. `LocResult(per_file: dict[str, int], max_loc: int)`), update the Tool-availability section to `AnalyzerResult(loc=LocResult(per_file={}, max_loc=0))`. If `max_loc` is a flat top-level field, update the AC prove-command to assert `.max_loc` not `.loc.max_loc`. Either way, add a one-line type signature for the relevant fields in the plan, or explicitly cite the 3b30 ticket's schema definition.

findings.finding: No test covers malformed or empty JSON output from scc (e.g., scc exits 0 but stdout is `''`, `'null'`, or truncated JSON). `json.loads` on empty/malformed input raises `JSONDecodeError`; `_scc_files([])` on empty list should yield `Unavailable` or `max_loc=0`. Neither path has an AC.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria â€” all three ACs; `scc_loc.py` boundary behavior

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
